Farn Pouucal PnacrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street e Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56@ . Fax (916) 322-0886
Iune7,2004
Ila Eileen Indelicato
Post Office Box 218
Lockeford, CA95237
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-04-104
Dear Ms. Indelicato
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter should not be
construed as advice on any decisions of the Lockeford Community Services District
Board of Directors which may have already taken place. Our assistance is based on the
facts presented in your request; the Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission',)
does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FppC
Ops.7l.)
QUESTIONS
In connection with the development of a new subdivision and anticipated grorvth
in the area proximate to the subdivision, may you participate in the deliberations of and
vote with the Lockeford Community Services District ("LCSD') Board of Directors
("Board') on the questions of whether to:2
1. Conduct an environmental study of property to be acquired for wastewater
disposal; and
2. Upgrade the LCSD sewer system, including the acquisition ofreal property for
wastewater disposal?
I Government Code sections 81000 - 9l}l4- Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
' Yo., also ask whether, generically, you will have a conflict of interest prohibiting you from
voting on issues relating to water and sewer service for future, unspecified counfy-approved developments.
This is a hlpothetical question which does not present an actual decision ofthe Board, based on a specific
proposal or known facts. We do not provide advice with respect to hypothetical questions and thus decline
to offer advice in response to this particular request. (See, regulation 1 8329(b)(8XD), copy enclosed.)
Re


File No. A-04-104
.Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. You may participate in the deliberations and vote of the Bomd with respect to
the decision of whether to conduct the enviroffiiental study. Since merely conduciing the
environmental study will no! by itself confer any benefits or permissions on the
subdivision developer, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to conduct the
sfudy will have a material financial effect upon one ormore of yorn economic interests.
2. You may not participate in the deliberations and yote ofthe Board with respect
to the upgrade of the LCSD sewer system and the acquisition ofreal property for
wastewater disposal facilities- Since you state that the subdivision, once completed,
could have a financial impact on your employer, it is reasonably foreseeable that these
decisions will have a material financial effect upon your employer, which is an economic
interest to you.
FACTS
LCSD is a commission for watetr, sewers and parks servicing an unincorporated
area of San Joaquin County. You are an elected member ofthe Board and will serve a
four-year term.
The San Joaquin County Planning Departnenthas approved amajor suffiivision
in an unincorporated area of the county which is tmgeted for growth under the county,s
general plan. Your principal residence is located app,roximat ely l/3 of a mile from the
proposed boundaries of the zuMivision. This area is zubject to the jurisdiction of the
LCSD and the developer of this subdivision receivod a'\rill serve" letter from the LCSD,
dated May 2003. The developer has requested that the LDSD upgrade the sewer facilities
which will service the subdivision. This will include thepurchase ofreal property to be
used as the site of additional wastewater disposal facilities. An enviromnental study will
be required in connection with the acquisition of any real property for this facility.
You are privately e,mployed as a real estate agent and work for a real estate
brokerage firm. Neith€r you nor your office will have any dealings with respect to
LCSD's negotiations for or purchase of additional wastewater disposal property. you
estimate that the new subdivision will not have a financial impact o, yo,, oi yo*
employer over the next two years. However, you anticipate that the new suMivision
would have a financial impact on you, as a real estate ag€nt, and on yourreal estate
brokerage firm employer, once the lots or homes are readyfor sale.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making participating in making or
otherwise using his orher official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifuing conflict ofinteresl
(regulation 18700(bxl) - (8). This standard analysis is discussed below.


FileNo. A-04-104
Page No. 3
l. & 2. Are you a public official making participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibitions apply only to public officials. As a member
of the Lockeford Community Services District Board ofDirectors, you are apublic
ofificial. (Section 82M8; regulation 13701(a).) As a directoq unless disqualified under
the conflict-of-interest provisions, you will make, participate in making o, or" yo.r,
official position to influence govemmental decisions ofthe Board, including roti.rg o1
the specific matters described in your request. (Reguration fi7a2J@)(1)-)t
3. What are your economic interests?
The economic interests which might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined
in regulations 187O3-18703.5. The specific economic interests that may apply to you are
described below: a
A. Business Entity
A public official has an economic interest in abusiness entityin which he or she
has a direct or indirect investnent of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or of which he or she is a director, officer, parhrer, trustee, employeg or
holds anyposition ofmanagement (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1Oi.) you are
employed by a real estate brokerage company. As an employee ofthis business, the real
estate brokerage is a business entity which is an economic interest to you.
B. Sources oflncome
A public official has an economic interest in any source of incomg including
promised incomg which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 871 03(c); regulation I 3703.3). Special rules apply to iaentifr sources
of income which are an economic interest to apublic official when the officiaireceives
3 If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 (*IZ2(0filers" include members of a
count5r's board ofsupervisors) and he or sh haq a conflict ofint€rost in a decision noticed at a public
meeting, then he or she must (l) irnnediatelypriorto the discussio,nofthe it€rn, \rqballyid€ndfi each
tlpe of economic interest involved in the dscision as well as &t2ils ofthe economic interest, as disc,ssed
in regulation 18702.5(bXl{B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself orherselt and (3) leave the
room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item- Forclosod sessions, "o*"rt cjendars,
absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules formd in
regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.) Since you-are a member of the
Lockeford Community Services District Board ofDirectors, these requirements apply to you.
- In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87l}3,a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities ofthe
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period. (Regulation
r 8703.5.)


File No. A-04-104
Page No. 4
commission income. Insofar as it relates to your circumstances, regulation
18703.3(c)(2XC) identifies these sources of income as follows:
"(C) A real estate agerfi:
(i) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices
the agent works;
(ii) The p€rson the agent represents in the transaction; and
(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for
referring aparty to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker."
Any of the foregoing persons who provide you, when acting in your capacrty as a real
estate agent, with income, includingpromisd income, which aggregates to $500 or more
within 12 months prior to a particular governmental decision will be an economic interest
to you.
C. Real Property
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2).
Presumably, your interest in the home which is your principal residence has a fair market
value of $2,000 or more. Thus, you have an economic interest in your principal
residence.
4. WiIl these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
A. Business Entity and Sources of Income - Any persoq including abusiness
entity, which is a source of income in which a public official has an economic interes! is
directly involved in a govemmental decision if that person, either directly or by an agent
initiates a proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similarreques! or is a
named party in, or is the subject of a procding before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation l87Aal@)(l)-(a)(2).) A business entity or source of income is the
subject of a proceeding concerning a decision before the official or the official's agency
if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the source of income.
(Regulation I 87(X. I ( a)(2).) If a business entity or source of income is not directly
involved in a govemmental decision, it is considered to be indirectly involved.
Under the facts you providg your real estate broker/employer is not the subject of
these decisions nor the initiator of the proceedings in which the decision will be made.
Thus, your real estate broker/employer is considered to be indirectly involved. Since we
do not have sufficient facts to identifu whether any of your sources of commission
income, other than your broker/ernployer, are directly or indirectly involved in these
decisions, you should apply the above regulations to determine whether any of these
sources are directly involved in these decisions.


File No. A-044A4
Page No. 5
B. Real Property - 
The appropriate standards for determining direct or indirect
involvement of an official's economic interest in real property are found in regulation
18704-2- Under this regulation, real property is considerod directly involved in a
govemmental decision if either: "l) [t]he real property in which the official has an
interest, or any part of that real property, is located within 50O feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental
decision-.." or the govemmental decision is of a tlpe which falls into any of the five
categories listed in regulation 187M.2(a)(2) - (a)(6). If a real property interest is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered to be indirectly involved for
the purpose of determining the appropriate materiality standard. (Regulation
t8704.2@)(2).)
In this instance, your principal residence is located approximately one-third of a
mile away from the subdivision (more than 500 feet from the bormdaries of the
subdivision) and does not meet any of the criteria under the regulations to be considerd
directly involved in these decisions. Thus, it is deemed indirectly involved in these
decisions.
5. & 6. \ilhat are the applicable materiality standards and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effects of the governmental decisions ulrcn your
economic interests will meet this materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effects of a govemmental decision on a public official's eoonomic interests are material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Ditrerent standards apply to detenrrine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial efilect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirecfly involved
in the agency's decision. We discuss below only the materiality standarrds that
correspond to the type of involvement identified in stq> 4, abova
A. Mateial Financial Effect
i. Business Entities
When a business entity that is an economic interest to a public official is
indirectly involved in a govemmental deeision, the financial effect deemed material
varies in amount in direct relationship to the size of the business entity- Regulation
18705.1(c) sets out four tiers which rank business emtities by size, using eith€r the
business entity's public listing status (i.e., among Fortune 500,listed on theNew York
Stock Exchange, American Stock Exchangg or NASDAQ) or, ifnot liste4 the business
entity's earnings before taxes (or in certain instances net income) over the most recent
fiscal year.
In a telephone conversation with Commission staffheld on June 1,2004,you
indicated that your brokerage/employer is not of sufficient size to be publicly traded or


FileNo. A-04-104
Page No. 6
meet the eligibility criteria to be publicly traded. Thus, due to its small size, this entity
falls under the ranking provided in subdivision 18705.1(c)(a). The materiality standard
appropriate to that ranking and applicable to this entity is:
"(A) The governmental decision will resuh in an increase or decrease
to the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 ormore; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in thebusiness €mtity
incurring or avoiding additional exp€nses or rducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amormt of $5,00o or more; or,
(C) The goveflrmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of business entity's assets or liabilities of $2O000 or more.',
(Regulation I 8705.1(c)(4XA) - (C).)
You indicate that this suMirrision could have an impact on you as a real estate
agent or upon yourbroker/employer- However, you also indicated in a June 1,2964,
telephone conversation with Commission staffthat the developer's spouse owns a real
estate brokerage firm in the community and is expected to carr5r the initial listings on any
properties sold within the development. Ultimately, the question becomes whether it is
reasonably foreseeable, under these circumstances, that these decisions will have any of
the above-listed financial effects on your employer. If so, the effect will be deemed
material and you will have a conflict of interest with respect to these decisions-
ii. Individuals as a Source of (Commission) Income
When a source of income (including commissions) to apublic official is an
individual who is indirectly involved in the governmental decision, the applicable
materiality standard is found at regulation 18705.3(b[3):
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's inoome, investnents, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $ 1,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material underTitle 2, california code of
Regulations, section I 8705.2(b)."
Thus, if it is reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will affect the incomg
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) of
individuals who provide you with commission income aggregating $500 ormore within
the 12 months preceding these decisions, the effect will be deemed material.
iii. Real Propetv
Under the Commission's regulations, it is presumed that a govemmental decision
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon real property which


FileNo. A-04-104
Page No. 7
is indirectly involved in that decision. (Regulations 18705.2(bxl) and OX2).) This
presumption may be rebutted under specific circumstances described in the
Commission's regulations. (Regulations ts705.z(b)(lxA)-(C), and 18705.z(b)(z)(A)-
(E).) Thus, it is presumed that these decisions will not have a material financial effect
upon your principal residence.
B. Reasonably Foreseeable
An effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeablg but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. t9S.)
i- Environmental Study: An envirorunental study is required under applicable
environmental protection/development laws. By itself, the study does not conf"" uny
licensg permit, or other authorization upon the developer to undertake any action in
furtherance of the subdivision project Nor does it express LCSD's appro-val of any facet
of the subdivision. Given these facts, there exists an "extra degree of remoteness,,
between decisions concerning the environmental study and any actions by the developer
which might have a financial affect upon your economic interests.s (NixonAdvice
Letter, No. 4-03-207.) Thus, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the Board,s decision
concerning the environmental study will have a material financial effect upon any of yogr
economic interests. Based on the facts you sq,ply, you do not have u *ofli"t ofinterest
prohibiting you from participating in the Bomd's vote on whether to authorize this study.
ii. Sewer Upgrades and Propertv Acquisition: These two decisions are vital
components of the subdivision project, the absence of which would undoubtdly mean
the subdivision project could not go forward. Moreover, the fact that these facilities
would enable sewer hookups for new development invariably adds value to the real estate
underlying the project in terms of assuring its development potential. Whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that affimrative decisions would inure to the benefit of,, or if the
decision were to go the other way, to the dehiment of, yourbroker/employer depends on
your assessnent of whether it is reasonably foreseeable under the totality of the
circumstances6 that the new development will have a material financial "tr""t,Ilon yonr
employer- The determination of what is reasonably foreseeable is a factual question and
as such, is ultimately for the public official, and not us, to make.
7- & 8. aPublic Generally' and (Legally Required Participation, Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
s In re Thorner corrcluded that one or more of the materiality standards applicable to a public
oflicial's economic interests will not be met if there exists an exfra degree ofremoteness between the
governmental decision and the decision's potential financial effect upon that economic interest- A copy of
this opinion is enclosed for your information.
6 This might include, for example, any exclusive listing agreement held by the developer,s spouse
in connection with the initial sale of subdivision properties.


File No. A-04-104
Page No. 8
effect on the public generally. (Section 83112; regulation rc7}7 ) Step eight is an
exception that applies when the official's participation in a governmental decision is
legally required. (Section 87101 ; regulation 18708.) Both of these narrowly construed
exceptions are fact-driven and you provide no facts addressing these exceptions.T
Accordingly, we offerno advice as to whether these exceptions aryly to you with respect
to the decisions you describe.
If you have any other questions regarding this matt€tr, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
By:
Kenneth
Glick
Counsel, Irgal Division
Enclosures
KLGje

7 There is a special form of the "public generally''exception which may apply when the decision
is made by the governing board of a water, irrigation, or similar district. (Regulation i*101.21"1.1
However, this special forrn of the exception is restricted to decisions regarding assessments, taxes, fees,
charges, or rates or other similar decisions, such as the allocation of services, rvm"n ure applied on a
propodional or 'across-the-board' basis" to both the official and ten percent or more of the property olyners
or other persons receiving services from the official's agency. You provide no facts indicating this special
form of the "public generally" exception applies to LCSD,s decisions.

sections.question:
In connection with the development of a new subdivision and anticipated grorvth
in the area proximate to the subdivision, may you participate in the deliberations of and
vote with the Lockeford Community Services District ("LCSD') Board of Directors
("Board') on the questions of whether to:2
1. Conduct an environmental study of property to be acquired for wastewater
disposal; and
2. Upgrade the LCSD sewer system, including the acquisition ofreal property for
wastewater disposal?
' Yo., also ask whether, generically, you will have a conflict of interest prohibiting you from
voting on issues relating to water and sewer service for future, unspecified counfy-approved developments.
This is a hlpothetical question which does not present an actual decision ofthe Board, based on a specific
proposal or known facts. We do not provide advice with respect to hypothetical questions and thus decline
to offer advice in response to this particular request. (See, regulation 1 8329(b)(8XD), copy enclosed.)
Re


.Page No. 2
sections.conclusion:
1. You may participate in the deliberations and vote of the Bomd with respect to
the decision of whether to conduct the enviroffiiental study. Since merely conduciing the
environmental study will no! by itself confer any benefits or permissions on the
subdivision developer, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to conduct the
sfudy will have a material financial effect upon one ormore of yorn economic interests.
2. You may not participate in the deliberations and yote ofthe Board with respect
to the upgrade of the LCSD sewer system and the acquisition ofreal property for
wastewater disposal facilities- Since you state that the subdivision, once completed,
could have a financial impact on your employer, it is reasonably foreseeable that these
decisions will have a material financial effect upon your employer, which is an economic
interest to you.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making participating in making or
otherwise using his orher official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard
analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifuing conflict ofinteresl
(regulation 18700(bxl) - (8). This standard analysis is discussed below.


FileNo. A-04-104
l. & 2. Are you a public official making participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest prohibitions apply only to public officials. As a member
of the Lockeford Community Services District Board ofDirectors, you are apublic
ofificial. (Section 82M8; regulation 13701(a).) As a directoq unless disqualified under
the conflict-of-interest provisions, you will make, participate in making o, or" yo.r,
official position to influence govemmental decisions ofthe Board, including roti.rg o1
the specific matters described in your request. (Reguration fi7a2J@)(1)-)t
3. What are your economic interests?
The economic interests which might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined
in regulations 187O3-18703.5. The specific economic interests that may apply to you are
described below: a
A. Business Entity
A public official has an economic interest in abusiness entityin which he or she
has a direct or indirect investnent of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or of which he or she is a director, officer, parhrer, trustee, employeg or
holds anyposition ofmanagement (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1Oi.) you are
employed by a real estate brokerage company. As an employee ofthis business, the real
estate brokerage is a business entity which is an economic interest to you.
B. Sources oflncome
A public official has an economic interest in any source of incomg including
promised incomg which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 871 03(c); regulation I 3703.3). Special rules apply to iaentifr sources
of income which are an economic interest to apublic official when the officiaireceives

in regulation 18702.5(bXl{B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself orherselt and (3) leave the
room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item- Forclosod sessions, "o*"rt cjendars,
absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules formd in

r 8703.5.)
commission income. Insofar as it relates to your circumstances, regulation
18703.3(c)(2XC) identifies these sources of income as follows:
"(C) A real estate agerfi:
(i) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices
the agent works;
(ii) The p€rson the agent represents in the transaction; and
(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for
referring aparty to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker."
Any of the foregoing persons who provide you, when acting in your capacrty as a real
estate agent, with income, includingpromisd income, which aggregates to $500 or more
within 12 months prior to a particular governmental decision will be an economic interest
to you.
C. Real Property
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2).
Presumably, your interest in the home which is your principal residence has a fair market
value of $2,000 or more. Thus, you have an economic interest in your principal
residence.
4. WiIl these economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
A. Business Entity and Sources of Income - Any persoq including abusiness
entity, which is a source of income in which a public official has an economic interes! is
directly involved in a govemmental decision if that person, either directly or by an agent
initiates a proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similarreques! or is a
named party in, or is the subject of a procding before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation l87Aal@)(l)-(a)(2).) A business entity or source of income is the
subject of a proceeding concerning a decision before the official or the official's agency
if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the source of income.
(Regulation I 87(X. I ( a)(2).) If a business entity or source of income is not directly
involved in a govemmental decision, it is considered to be indirectly involved.
Under the facts you providg your real estate broker/employer is not the subject of
these decisions nor the initiator of the proceedings in which the decision will be made.
Thus, your real estate broker/employer is considered to be indirectly involved. Since we
do not have sufficient facts to identifu whether any of your sources of commission
income, other than your broker/ernployer, are directly or indirectly involved in these
decisions, you should apply the above regulations to determine whether any of these
sources are directly involved in these decisions.


File No. A-044A4
B. Real Property - 
The appropriate standards for determining direct or indirect
involvement of an official's economic interest in real property are found in regulation
18704-2- Under this regulation, real property is considerod directly involved in a
govemmental decision if either: "l) [t]he real property in which the official has an
interest, or any part of that real property, is located within 50O feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental
decision-.." or the govemmental decision is of a tlpe which falls into any of the five
categories listed in regulation 187M.2(a)(2) - (a)(6). If a real property interest is not
directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered to be indirectly involved for
the purpose of determining the appropriate materiality standard. (Regulation
t8704.2@)(2).)
In this instance, your principal residence is located approximately one-third of a
mile away from the subdivision (more than 500 feet from the bormdaries of the
subdivision) and does not meet any of the criteria under the regulations to be considerd
directly involved in these decisions. Thus, it is deemed indirectly involved in these
decisions.
5. & 6. \ilhat are the applicable materiality standards and is it reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effects of the governmental decisions ulrcn your
economic interests will meet this materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effects of a govemmental decision on a public official's eoonomic interests are material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Ditrerent standards apply to detenrrine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial efilect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirecfly involved
in the agency's decision. We discuss below only the materiality standarrds that
correspond to the type of involvement identified in stq> 4, abova
A. Mateial Financial Effect
i. Business Entities
When a business entity that is an economic interest to a public official is
indirectly involved in a govemmental deeision, the financial effect deemed material
varies in amount in direct relationship to the size of the business entity- Regulation
18705.1(c) sets out four tiers which rank business emtities by size, using eith€r the
business entity's public listing status (i.e., among Fortune 500,listed on theNew York
Stock Exchange, American Stock Exchangg or NASDAQ) or, ifnot liste4 the business
entity's earnings before taxes (or in certain instances net income) over the most recent
fiscal year.
In a telephone conversation with Commission staffheld on June 1,2004,you
indicated that your brokerage/employer is not of sufficient size to be publicly traded or


FileNo. A-04-104
meet the eligibility criteria to be publicly traded. Thus, due to its small size, this entity
falls under the ranking provided in subdivision 18705.1(c)(a). The materiality standard
appropriate to that ranking and applicable to this entity is:
"(A) The governmental decision will resuh in an increase or decrease
to the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 ormore; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in thebusiness €mtity
incurring or avoiding additional exp€nses or rducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amormt of $5,00o or more; or,
(C) The goveflrmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of business entity's assets or liabilities of $2O000 or more.',
(Regulation I 8705.1(c)(4XA) - (C).)
You indicate that this suMirrision could have an impact on you as a real estate
agent or upon yourbroker/employer- However, you also indicated in a June 1,2964,
telephone conversation with Commission staffthat the developer's spouse owns a real
estate brokerage firm in the community and is expected to carr5r the initial listings on any
properties sold within the development. Ultimately, the question becomes whether it is
reasonably foreseeable, under these circumstances, that these decisions will have any of
the above-listed financial effects on your employer. If so, the effect will be deemed
material and you will have a conflict of interest with respect to these decisions-
ii. Individuals as a Source of (Commission) Income
When a source of income (including commissions) to apublic official is an
individual who is indirectly involved in the governmental decision, the applicable
materiality standard is found at regulation 18705.3(b[3):
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's inoome, investnents, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $ 1,000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material underTitle 2, california code of
Regulations, section I 8705.2(b)."
Thus, if it is reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will affect the incomg
investments, or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) of
individuals who provide you with commission income aggregating $500 ormore within
the 12 months preceding these decisions, the effect will be deemed material.
iii. Real Propetv
Under the Commission's regulations, it is presumed that a govemmental decision
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon real property which


FileNo. A-04-104
is indirectly involved in that decision. (Regulations 18705.2(bxl) and OX2).) This
presumption may be rebutted under specific circumstances described in the
Commission's regulations. (Regulations ts705.z(b)(lxA)-(C), and 18705.z(b)(z)(A)-
(E).) Thus, it is presumed that these decisions will not have a material financial effect
upon your principal residence.
B. Reasonably Foreseeable
An effect upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeablg but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. t9S.)
i- Environmental Study: An envirorunental study is required under applicable
environmental protection/development laws. By itself, the study does not conf"" uny
licensg permit, or other authorization upon the developer to undertake any action in
furtherance of the subdivision project Nor does it express LCSD's appro-val of any facet
of the subdivision. Given these facts, there exists an "extra degree of remoteness,,
between decisions concerning the environmental study and any actions by the developer
which might have a financial affect upon your economic interests.s (NixonAdvice
Letter, No. 4-03-207.) Thus, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the Board,s decision
concerning the environmental study will have a material financial effect upon any of yogr
economic interests. Based on the facts you sq,ply, you do not have u *ofli"t ofinterest
prohibiting you from participating in the Bomd's vote on whether to authorize this study.
ii. Sewer Upgrades and Propertv Acquisition: These two decisions are vital
components of the subdivision project, the absence of which would undoubtdly mean
the subdivision project could not go forward. Moreover, the fact that these facilities
would enable sewer hookups for new development invariably adds value to the real estate
underlying the project in terms of assuring its development potential. Whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that affimrative decisions would inure to the benefit of,, or if the
decision were to go the other way, to the dehiment of, yourbroker/employer depends on
your assessnent of whether it is reasonably foreseeable under the totality of the
circumstances6 that the new development will have a material financial "tr""t,Ilon yonr
employer- The determination of what is reasonably foreseeable is a factual question and
as such, is ultimately for the public official, and not us, to make.
7- & 8. aPublic Generally' and (Legally Required Participation, Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
s In re Thorner corrcluded that one or more of the materiality standards applicable to a public
oflicial's economic interests will not be met if there exists an exfra degree ofremoteness between the
governmental decision and the decision's potential financial effect upon that economic interest- A copy of
this opinion is enclosed for your information.
6 This might include, for example, any exclusive listing agreement held by the developer,s spouse
in connection with the initial sale of subdivision properties.
effect on the public generally. (Section 83112; regulation rc7}7 ) Step eight is an
exception that applies when the official's participation in a governmental decision is
legally required. (Section 87101 ; regulation 18708.) Both of these narrowly construed
exceptions are fact-driven and you provide no facts addressing these exceptions.T
Accordingly, we offerno advice as to whether these exceptions aryly to you with respect
to the decisions you describe.

QUESTION: In connection with the development of a new subdivision and anticipated grorvth
in the area proximate to the subdivision, may you participate in the deliberations of and
vote with the Lockeford Community Services District ("LCSD') Board of Directors
("Board') on the questions of whether to:2
1. Conduct an environmental study of property to be acquired for wastewater
disposal; and
2. Upgrade the LCSD sewer system, including the acquisition ofreal property for
wastewater disposal?
' Yo., also ask whether, generically, you will have a conflict of interest prohibiting you from
voting on issues relating to water and sewer service for future, unspecified counfy-approved developments.
This is a hlpothetical question which does not present an actual decision ofthe Board, based on a specific
proposal or known facts. We do not provide advice with respect to hypothetical questions and thus decline
to offer advice in response to this particular request. (See, regulation 1 8329(b)(8XD), copy enclosed.)
Re


.Page No. 2

CONCLUSION: 1. You may participate in the deliberations and vote of the Bomd with respect to
the decision of whether to conduct the enviroffiiental study. Since merely conduciing the
environmental study will no! by itself confer any benefits or permissions on the
subdivision developer, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision to conduct the
sfudy will have a material financial effect upon one ormore of yorn economic interests.
2. You may not participate in the deliberations and yote ofthe Board with respect
to the upgrade of the LCSD sewer system and the acquisition ofreal property for
wastewater disposal facilities- Since you state that the subdivision, once completed,
could have a financial impact on your employer, it is reasonably foreseeable that these
decisions will have a material financial effect upon your employer, which is an economic
interest to you.
